Adam Clarke notes Jacob says they have troubled him, brought his mind into great distress, endangered his safety, and made him to stink, to be abhorred, among the people of the land. The witnesses note Jacob rebuking Simeon and Levi for making him odious among the Canaanites, who might gather and destroy his house; the grieved rebuke weighing the deadly peril — that Jacob, returning to find the bloody work done, sharply reproves the two: "Ye have troubled me to make me to stink among the inhabitants of the land... and I being few in number, they shall gather themselves together against me, and slay me; and I shall be destroyed, I and my house"; the witnesses note the substance of the rebuke — Jacob charges them that they have "troubled" him (brought him into great distress and danger) and made him "to stink" (odious and abhorred) "among the Canaanites and the Perizzites," the peoples of the land; and he fears the consequence, that, "being few in number," he and his house would be no match for the surrounding nations, who might now "gather... together against" them to take vengeance, and "slay" and "destroy" them all; the witnesses note that some have observed Jacob's rebuke dwells on the danger and dishonour more than on the sin and cruelty themselves; the witnesses note that his fear was real, yet God would protect him (the next chapter shows a terror of God falling on the cities); so that Jacob rebukes his sons for the deadly peril their cruelty had brought; I shall be destroyed, I and my house. The witnesses note Ye have troubled me to make me to stink among the inhabitants of the land. The witnesses note they shall gather themselves together against me, and slay me; and I shall be destroyed, I and my house.

The witnesses note Jacob's grieved rebuke — that Simeon and Levi had made him odious among the peoples and brought deadly peril on his few household; some marking that he dwells on the danger more than on the cruelty itself.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob's rebuke of his sons' deadly deed. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob reproved Simeon and Levi for making him odious among the nations and endangering his whole house. "And they said, Should he deal with our sister as with an harlot?" The witnesses note the brothers' defiant justification of their deed. Gill notes they answered in a pert and unseemly manner: should he make a whore of their sister, and then keep her in his house as such, and is this to be borne? The witnesses note Simeon and Levi answering that Shechem should not have dealt with their sister as a harlot; the brothers' bold self-justification, pleading the wrong as warrant for the whole revenge — that the chapter closes with the brothers' unrepentant reply: "Should he deal with our sister as with an harlot?"; the witnesses note the spirit of the answer — Simeon and Levi, far from owning any fault, justify themselves with a defiant question, pleading the foulness of the wrong done Dinah: should Shechem be suffered to treat "our sister as with an harlot," to violate her and then keep her, as if she were a common woman, and the family bear it tamely?; the witnesses note the real grievance beneath the defiance — that the wrong was indeed grievous, and the sense of their sister's dishonour was natural; yet the witnesses note that this just feeling was no answer to Jacob's charge, for it could not justify the treachery, the massacre of a whole city, and the plunder of the innocent; the witnesses note that the brothers had the better of the grievance but not of the cause, a real wrong avenged in a wicked way; so that the brothers close with a defiant plea that excused not their cruelty; Should he deal with our sister as with an harlot? The witnesses note And they said. The witnesses note Should he deal with our sister as with an harlot?

The witnesses note the brothers' defiant self-justification — pleading the foul wrong to Dinah as warrant for the revenge; a real grievance, yet no answer to Jacob's charge, for it could not justify the treachery and slaughter.
